# Phase 2: non-interactive auth via NLM_AUTH_TOKEN / NLM_COOKIES.
#
# These tests verify the "fresh CI machine" story documented in the
# release plan — a user can go straight from `go install` to running
# commands by exporting two environment variables, without `nlm auth`
# ever launching a browser or touching a local profile.

# `auth --print-env` emits exactly two `export` lines on stdout
# and nothing else. Diagnostics go to stderr.
env NLM_AUTH_TOKEN=tok-123
env NLM_COOKIES=SIDabc_HSIDxyz
exec ./nlm_test auth --print-env
stdout 'export NLM_AUTH_TOKEN=''tok-123'''
stdout 'export NLM_COOKIES=''SIDabc_HSIDxyz'''
! stdout 'nlm:'
! stdout 'launching'

# `auth --print-env` fails actionably when there is nothing to print.
env NLM_AUTH_TOKEN=
env NLM_COOKIES=
! exec ./nlm_test auth --print-env
stderr 'no authenticated session found'
stderr 'NLM_AUTH_TOKEN'
stderr 'NLM_COOKIES'

# With env vars set but no profile on disk, the auth-required error
# still names both paths so the user knows which lever to pull.
env NLM_AUTH_TOKEN=
env NLM_COOKIES=
! exec ./nlm_test list
stderr 'Authentication required'
stderr 'nlm auth'
stderr 'NLM_AUTH_TOKEN'
stderr 'NLM_COOKIES'
stderr 'print-env'

# Env-var-only mode never retries browser auth on a bad token — the
# single attempt returns the session-expired hint instead of chaining
# into Chrome. A bogus token hits the real API (this txtar runs
# offline-only, so what matters is the exit is non-zero and no
# authentication loop output appears).
env NLM_AUTH_TOKEN=bad-token
env NLM_COOKIES=bad-cookie
! exec ./nlm_test list
! stderr 'launching browser'
! stderr 'retrying authentication'
